1:
The book's last chapter opens with the siege that will end everything, and the witnesses read the bare date — the ninth year, the tenth month, the tenth day of Zedekiah's reign — as the hinge on which Judah's ruin turns. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the scene against the king's folly: we left Zedekiah in rebellion against the king of Babylon, contriving to shake off a yoke he had no strength to break and no wisdom to escape, for he never took the right course of making God his friend first (2 Kings 24:20). Now come the fatal consequences. Nebuchadnezzar's army pitched against Jerusalem and built forts round about it, whence by such arts of war as they then had they battered the walls and shut out the necessary supports of life. Formerly, Henry says, Jerusalem had been compassed with the favour of God as with a shield; but now that defence had departed, and their enemies surrounded them on every side — for those who by sin provoke God to leave them find that innumerable evils compass them about.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fixes the chronology with care, comparing the parallel accounts in Jer. 52:4ff and Jer. 39:1-10, and noting that the exact date agrees with Ezek. 24:1-2, where the same day was revealed to the prophet in exile. These days, he observes, were afterward kept as fasts through the captivity (Zech. 7:3). Nebuchadnezzar came against Jerusalem only after reducing the other strong places of Judah, as Jer. 34:7 records; and the "forts" are a siege-work whose Hebrew name recurs in Ezek. 4:2, rendered "wall" by the Septuagint and "palisade" by the Syriac.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the two verbs morally: the Chaldean "came" to chastise Zedekiah for his rebellion and perjury, and "built" his forts to keep every supply of men and provisions from entering the city, and to hurl from them darts and arrows and stones. Adam Clarke, of the same school, frames the whole chapter in advance — the siege, the famine, the king's flight and capture, his blinding and chains — as the settled purpose of a king wearied by Judah's treachery. The reformed John Gill adds only that from this verse to the end the narrative agrees exactly with the fuller record of Jeremiah, so that the historian and the prophet speak with one voice over the city's fall. Together the witnesses make the date itself a sermon: the long patience of God is closing, and the shield is withdrawn.
8:
A new agent enters — "in the fifth month, on the seventh day of the month... came Nebuzaradan, captain of the guard, a servant of the king of Babylon, unto Jerusalem" — and the witnesses weigh both the date and the man. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch treat the verse as the heading of the whole destruction to come: first the general account of the burning of temple and city, then the carrying away of the people, then the fate of the vessels and of the captured leaders, all wrought by Nebuzaradan, the chief of Nebuchadnezzar's bodyguard, in the nineteenth year of his reign (Jer. 52:12). Charles Ellicott, of the same school, unravels the name and the office: Nebuzaradan represents the Babylonian Nabu-zir-iddina, "Nebo gave seed," and "captain of the guard" means strictly chief of the executioners, commander of the royal bodyguard, the Praetorians of that court (Genesis 37:36). He notes a scribal problem in the date: the "seventh day" here is likely an error for the "tenth" of Jer. 52:12, one numeral letter mistaken for another, while the Syriac reads "ninth"; and since a whole month elapsed between the breaching of the city and its burning, some infer that the temple mount held out under a determined defence until this distinguished commander was sent to end it.

The reformed John Gill notes only the same discrepancy of the day, referring the reader to the harmonizing comment on Jeremiah. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the timetable: Nebuzaradan entered the city on the seventh, spent two days making provision, and on the tenth set fire to the temple, the palace, and the houses of the nobles — so that the temple perished in the eleventh year of Zedekiah, the nineteenth of Nebuchadnezzar, some four hundred and twenty-four years from the laying of Solomon's foundation stone.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds a mercy hidden in the interval. Though the Chaldean army was enraged at the city's stubborn resistance, they did not put all to fire and sword the moment it fell; about a month passed before Nebuzaradan came with orders to complete its ruin. That space, Henry says, God gave them to repent, after all the foregoing days of his patience — but in vain, for their hearts, so far as appears, were still hardened, and therefore execution was at last awarded to the utmost. John Wesley reads the delay in the same way, as one more offer of the patience of God, refused. The witnesses do not divide: the coming of the captain of the guard is, in their common reading, the arrival of a long-deferred judgment, exact in its dating and terrible in its office, upon a city that would not turn even when the axe was already at the root.
13:
"And the pillars of brass that were in the house of the LORD, and the bases, and the brasen sea... did the Chaldees break in pieces, and carried the brass of them to Babylon." The witnesses turn from people to the sacred furniture, and read its destruction as a judgment on a worship long profaned. The reformed John Gill identifies the pillars as Jachin and Boaz, and cannot resist the traveller's legend he has met: Benjamin of Tudela reported that these very pillars stood in the church of St. Stephen at Rome, engraved with Solomon's name, and that the Jews there claimed they sweated moisture every year on the ninth of Ab, the day the temple was destroyed — a tale, Gill dryly implies, as credible as it is marvellous, since the text plainly says the Chaldeans broke them in pieces. He notes that from here the account runs parallel with the fuller record of Jeremiah, where a chapiter's height is given as five cubits against the three named here — a discrepancy he leaves to his note on that place.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott marks that from this point the shorter narrative of Jer. 39 ceases to run parallel, and refers the reader for these objects to the original description in 1 Kings 7:15 and following; the metal called "brass," he adds, should throughout be understood as copper. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, explain why such splendid things were shattered rather than carried whole: the pillars, the stands, and the great brazen sea of 1 Kings 7:23 were colossal, and it would have been hard to move them without breaking them up; the smaller vessels are catalogued in the verses that follow.

The witnesses do not divide over the fact, and beneath their technical notes lies a shared sense of loss and of justice. The two great pillars that had stood at the porch of Solomon's house, whose very names — "He shall establish" and "In him is strength" — proclaimed the stability of the house of God, are broken up for scrap and hauled away as so much metal. What the puritan reading of these chapters calls the sinning-away of the things signified is worked out here in bronze: when the reality had been forsaken, the costly signs of it could not stand. The Chaldean hammers that reduce Jachin and Boaz to fragments are, in the witnesses' reading, the last act of a long desecration begun within, by the people whose idolatry had already emptied these symbols of their meaning.
18:
"And the captain of the guard took Seraiah the chief priest, and Zephaniah the second priest, and the three keepers of the door." The witnesses turn from the vessels to the men who had served among them, the temple's highest officers led off to death. The reformed John Gill explains the office of the "second priest" as the sagan or deputy, who officiated for the high priest whenever infirmity or defilement kept him from the service — a substitute regularly appointed, he notes, in the days before the Day of Atonement, lest anything should incapacitate the high priest at that solemn time. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees that Zephaniah the second priest was this sagan, a kind of deputy who performed the high priest's functions in his stead, and refers back to the same term used in 2 Kings 23:4.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds the genealogy and rank. Seraiah the chief priest was the grandfather, or great-grandfather, of Ezra, as the line in Ezra 7:1 shows; Zephaniah, named elsewhere as a priest of high standing in Jer. 21:1 and Jer. 37:3, was probably the high priest's deputy; and the "three keepers of the door," or of the threshold, were the chief warders of the principal entrances to the temple. It is plain, Ellicott observes, that all the chief officials of the sanctuary were carried off together.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, numbering the notable men taken and slain in cold blood, sets Seraiah the chief priest — father, as he too notes, of the line of Ezra — and the second priest and the three door-keepers at the head of the list, and reads the whole company as marked out by a double justice: the king of Babylon's revenge singled them out as leaders in the resistance, but divine justice, Henry suggests, looked upon them rather as ringleaders in the idolatry and impiety that had brought on these desolations. The witnesses do not divide. Together they show that the sword did not fall at random: it fell first on the men who had stood nearest the altar, the high priest, his deputy, and the warders of the holy doors. There is a grave fitness in it that the witnesses feel keenly — that the ministers of a worship long corrupted should be the first to answer for it, and that the sanctuary emptied of its vessels should now be emptied of its priests, so that both the service and its servants pass together into the judgment their unfaithfulness had earned.
22:
"And as for the people that remained... over them he made Gedaliah the son of Ahikam, the son of Shaphan, ruler." With the kingdom fallen, the witnesses gather around the good man set to govern its ashes. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace his lineage and his character: Gedaliah was son of that Ahikam who had once shielded the prophet Jeremiah and saved his life (Jer. 26:24), and grandson of Shaphan the scribe; a man whose upright conduct through the siege had, they judge, won the confidence of the Chaldeans, so that Nebuchadnezzar entrusted to him the oversight of the poor and the remnant left behind. He fixed his residence at Mizpah, which they identify with Nebi Samwil, two hours northwest of Jerusalem.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, presses the vindication of Gedaliah's honour. His father Ahikam had been one of Josiah's princes and had rescued Jeremiah from the popular fury; Nebuzaradan committed the prophet to Gedaliah's care (Jer. 39:13), and Gedaliah, like his father, plainly shared Jeremiah's views. His magnanimous conduct, Ellicott insists, shows he was no traitor or deserter, as some have miscalled him, but a disciple of the prophet who did his utmost to lead the remnant to submit with patience to their divinely appointed lot.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry sets the appointment in the light of mercy and warning together. Some in the land of Judah had weathered the storm and, as a great favour at such a time, had their lives given them for a prey (Jer. 45:5); over them the king of Babylon set one of their own, a very good man who would make the best of the bad, having beside him Jeremiah himself for a kind of privy-council. Gedaliah, Henry says, though he had not the pomp of a king, might have been a greater blessing to them than many of their kings had been. The reformed John Gill notes that the scattered captains submitted to him for a time, while the wesleyan-arminian voices concur — Adam Clarke observing that his was no regal dignity but the office of an overseer set over the husbandmen, and John Wesley calling him simply a righteous and good man, a friend to the prophet Jeremiah. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that in the wreck of the kingdom God left a good governor and a living prophet together, a fair prospect of quiet under Babylon — a mercy, as the coming verses will show, that the remnant would not have the wisdom to keep.
25:
"But it came to pass in the seventh month, that Ishmael the son of Nethaniah... of the seed royal, came, and ten men with him, and smote Gedaliah, that he died." The witnesses recount the murder that undid the last settlement, and probe the assassin's motive. The reformed John Gill fixes the time: the seventh month not of Gedaliah's brief government but of the year, the month Tisri, hardly two months after the destruction of Jerusalem — the Jews reckon fifty-two days after it. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in the treachery from the fuller account: Ishmael came with his ten men and murdered Gedaliah together with the Jews and Chaldean soldiers who were with him, and this after the governor had received him hospitably and set food before him, as Jer. 41:1 relates. Ishmael had been incited to the deed by Baalis the Ammonite king, and Gedaliah had even been warned of the plot by Johanan but had refused to believe it (Jer. 40:14).

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, finds the motive in the assassin's blood: being "of the seed royal," Ishmael thought his own claim to govern the remnant greater than Gedaliah's, and struck at a friendly meal, when, as Josephus adds, the company were overcome with wine. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke enlarges the crime: not content with this murder, Ishmael slew fourscore more who were coming with offerings toward the temple, took captives including some of the king's daughters, and set off toward the Ammonites, until Johanan pursued him and his prisoners turned and joined the pursuer, so that Ishmael escaped with but eight men. John Wesley adds that Ishmael was moved by envy, to see so mean a person as Gedaliah advanced into what he thought his own place, and that the "ten men" were captains each with soldiers under him.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they turn the bare notice of a death into a study of wanton self-destruction. Everything that mercy had left to Judah — a good governor, a living prophet, peace under Babylon, the freedom to till their own fields — is thrown away in a single act of jealous violence by one of her own royal house. The grammatical-historical witnesses expose the motive of a pretender's pride; the wesleyan-arminian witnesses count the widening slaughter that followed. All see in it the bitter irony of the whole book: what the Chaldean sword had spared, a son of David's line destroys; and the day of Gedaliah's murder, long kept afterward as a fast, becomes the seal upon Judah's inability to receive even the mercies that survived her ruin.
26:
"And all the people, both small and great, and the captains of the armies, arose, and came to Egypt: for they were afraid of the Chaldees." The witnesses read the flight into Egypt as the last and most needless self-ruin of the remnant. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set it in its sequel: after Ishmael's crime and his slaughter of the pilgrims from Shechem and Shiloh and Samaria, he was pursued by Johanan, overtaken at Gibeon, and stripped of his captives, escaping at last with only eight men to the Ammonites (Jer. 41:11-15); whereupon Johanan and the people, gathering near Bethlehem, resolved to flee to Egypt for fear of Chaldean reprisal, and though they sought a word from Jeremiah, they would not obey it when it came. Charles Ellicott notes drily that this verse only gives the end of the story told at length in Jeremiah, and that they took the prophet Jeremiah down with them (Jer. 43:6).

The reformed John Gill observes that among these fugitives were the king's daughters who had been in Gedaliah's care, and the prophets Jeremiah and Baruch, carried along with the rest. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the melancholy conclusion: in Egypt they probably mingled with the Egyptians by degrees and were heard of no more as Israelites. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already pressed the folly of the flight. They were still in their own good land, yet they forsook it; the Chaldeans had reason enough to resent the murder of Gedaliah, but had the innocent remnant simply pleaded that it was Ishmael's act, they might well have been spared. Instead, against the express counsel of Jeremiah, they all went down to Egypt — and so, Henry says, a full end was made of them by their own folly and disobedience, the very last of the threats fulfilled, that the LORD should bring them into Egypt again (Deut. 28:68).

The witnesses do not divide. Between Keil's narrative, Ellicott's cross-reference, Gill's roll of the captives, and Henry's and Wesley's lament, they make the descent into Egypt the mournful mirror of Israel's whole history reversed. The people whom God had once brought up out of Egypt with a mighty hand now flee back into it in fear, dragging the prophet with them and leaving their own land empty behind. What might have been a quiet remnant under Gedaliah scatters to the very house of bondage their fathers had left, and the book's long record of disobedience closes, fittingly, with a flight in the wrong direction — away from the land of promise, away from the word of God, into the land of their first slavery.
27:
The book's final scene lifts, unexpectedly, toward light — "in the seven and thirtieth year of the captivity of Jehoiachin king of Judah... Evil-merodach king of Babylon... did lift up the head of Jehoiachin king of Judah out of prison." The witnesses read this closing note as a deliberate gleam of hope after so much darkness. The reformed John Gill reckons that Jehoiachin must now have been fifty-five years old. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the timing and the idiom: it was "in the year of his becoming king" that Evil-merodach did this, most likely as an act of grace at the very outset of his reign; and "to lift up the head" means to release a man from prison and raise him to honour, the same phrase used of Pharaoh's butler in Genesis 40:13. They note that Jeremiah gives the twenty-fifth day where Kings has the twenty-seventh.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, supplies the chronicle. Evil-merodach — in Babylonian Amil-marduk, "man of Merodach" — came to the throne on the death of Nebuchadnezzar, who had reigned forty-three years; by the canon of Ptolemy he ruled but two years, and was murdered by his brother-in-law Neriglissar. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the release in the same frame: Nebuchadnezzar being just dead, his son succeeded, and on that day of the twelfth month brought the long-captive Jewish king out of prison, treated him kindly, and ever after reckoned him among the king's friends, all of which, he adds, is told in the last four verses of the book of Jeremiah. John Wesley resolves the discrepancy of the dates, supposing the decree was made on the twenty-fifth and executed on the twenty-seventh.

The witnesses do not divide. Together they mark the strangeness and the mercy of the moment: after thirty-seven years in a Babylonian prison, the last acknowledged king of David's line is brought out, his head lifted, his fortunes turned, by the hand of a heathen successor. The grammatical-historical witnesses fix the names and numbers; the wesleyan-arminian witnesses stress the kindness of the act. All sense that the historian has not chosen this ending by accident. The book that has moved relentlessly through apostasy, siege, burning, and exile does not close on the ashes of Jerusalem or the flight into Egypt, but on a prison door swung open and a captive king raised to favour. It is a small thing against so vast a ruin — yet, as the following verses will make plain, it is set here as a token that the line of David is not extinguished, and that the God who has kept his word of judgment has not forgotten his word of promise.
2:
"And the city was besieged unto the eleventh year of king Zedekiah" — the witnesses dwell on the sheer length of the ordeal and what it reveals about the city and its doom. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch reckon the siege at some eighteen months, and take its very duration as proof of how strong the fortifications of Jerusalem must have been to hold out so long against the whole might of Babylon. They note too that the blockade was not unbroken: it was interrupted for a time when the approach of the Egyptian king Hophra compelled the Chaldeans to march away and meet him, driving him back, it seems, without a pitched battle — an episode referred to in Jer. 37:5 and Ezek. 17:7.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, does the fuller arithmetic. Comparing the opening date of the siege with the month of the city's destruction, he counts the whole affliction at one year, five months, and twenty-seven days. He confirms Keil's point about the lifted siege, that the Chaldeans raised it for a season and marched against Pharaoh-Hophra, who was coming up to the help of the Jews — the very Egyptian succour on which Zedekiah had gambled — citing the same prophetic notices in Jer. 37:5 and the oracles against Egypt in Ezek. 17:17 and Ezek. 30:20.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke narrates the sequel that turned the brief reprieve into a doom. Nebuchadnezzar, having routed the Egyptian army, returned and pressed the siege so closely that the city, reduced by famine and breached at last in its wall, was entered by the Chaldeans on the ninth day of the fourth month — a Wednesday near the end of July, he reckons — while Zedekiah and many with him tried to steal away by night.

The witnesses do not divide. They converge on a single sober picture: a city so mightily walled that only an eighteen-month strangulation could take it; a brief false dawn when Egypt seemed to be marching to its rescue; and then the return of the besieger to finish what he had begun. The strength of the walls, in their reading, only measures the strength of the judgment that at last broke through them; and the Egyptian alliance on which the king had staked his throne proved, exactly as the prophets had warned, a broken reed that pierced the hand which leaned on it. The long resistance was real, but it was resistance to the inevitable — the outward strength of Jerusalem prolonging, but never averting, the fall that her inward sin had made sure.
3:
The verse marks the day the siege became unendurable — "on the ninth day of the fourth month the famine prevailed in the city, and there was no bread for the people of the land" — and the witnesses read the famine as both the mechanism and the meaning of the fall. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch trace it to the king's obstinacy: trusting partly to the help of the Egyptians and partly to the strength of Jerusalem, Zedekiah paid no heed to Jeremiah's repeated entreaties that he save himself and his people by submitting to the Chaldeans (Jer. 38:17), but let matters run to their worst, until the famine grew so intense that inhuman horrors were perpetrated (Lam. 4:9-10). They observe a textual point: the naming of the fourth month has dropped out of the Kings text through a copyist's oversight and must be supplied from Jer. 52:6; and they insist the famine is recorded not merely to explain why resistance collapsed but as a proof that the prophetic word had come true.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, presses the horror further. The scarcity was not first felt on that day but then reached its climax, so that defence was no longer possible; the miseries of the siege are those bewailed in Lam. 2:11 and Lam. 4:3-10, when, as in the earlier famine of Samaria, parents ate their own offspring — the very curse threatened long before in Lev. 26:29 and Deut. 28:53. Against Thenius, who wanted "the people of the land" to mean the militia, Ellicott holds that the phrase means the civil population of the city, especially the families who had crowded in from the country, since the soldiers are separately named as the "men of war" in v.4.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees that it was the common people, not the great men, who first felt the want, and that they came to eating their own children for lack of food, as Lam. 4:3 laments. He returns, as Keil had, to the king's fatal deafness: Jeremiah in this extremity earnestly persuaded Zedekiah to surrender, but his heart was hardened to his own destruction. The witnesses thus make the famine a parable of the whole reign — a city that would not yield to the word of God is at last brought to yield to hunger, and the bread that fails within the walls is the visible sign of a mercy long refused.
4:
"And the city was broken up" — the wall is breached at last, and the witnesses follow the doomed flight of the king into the night. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reconstructs the assault: a breach was made in the wall with battering-rams such as the Assyrian sculptures depict, and the Chaldeans forced their entry on the north side, taking the lower city, after which their generals pressed on to the "middle gate," as Jer. 39:3 relates. He notes that the Hebrew of the verse is defective, wanting a verb and the express mention of the king, which the parallels in Jer. 39:4 and Jer. 52:7 allow us to restore. The escape ran "by the way of the gate between the two walls, which is by the king's garden" — a gate at the south end of the Tyropoeon valley, the same he identifies with the Gate of the Fountain in Neh. 3:15, the double wall having been built to guard the water supply at the Pool of Siloam. Since the Chaldees lay round about the city, Ellicott stresses that even by this route the king had to break through the besiegers' lines, exactly as Ezek. 12:12 had foreseen; and "the way toward the plain" he identifies as the Arabah, the valley of the Jordan.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, carry the flight to its end. Because the Chaldeans were encamped all around, the escape was discovered at once; their army pursued and overtook the king in the steppes of Jericho, that portion of the Jordan plain surrounding the city, while his own army was dispersed and scattered from him — all of which, they note, Ezekiel had already seen in the Spirit and declared in Ezek. 12:3.

The two witnesses do not differ; between them they turn the terse notice into a vivid scene of collapse. The wall that had held for a year and a half is at last pierced; the king who would not walk out to the Chaldeans in surrender now creeps out in the dark, only to be run down in the open plain. The very secrecy of the flight, in their reading, could not outrun the word of the prophets, who had foretold both the breach and the capture; and the scattering of his guard shows how a throne that had leaned on Egypt and its own walls is left, in the hour of need, with nothing between it and the judgment of God. The gate by the king's garden, meant for a quiet royal walk beside the pool, becomes the door of a desperate escape; and the plain that opens toward freedom becomes the field of his capture.
6:
"So they took the king, and brought him up to the king of Babylon to Riblah; and they gave judgment upon him." The witnesses fix their attention on the strange formality of the scene — a captured king put on trial — and on what the trial was for. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that Zedekiah, once seized, was carried to Nebuchadnezzar's headquarters at Riblah, the same northern station named in 2 Kings 23:33, and there brought to trial. Nebuchadnezzar, they note, had opened the siege in person but afterward, perhaps once the relieving Egyptians had been repulsed, handed its long continuance to his generals and withdrew to Riblah to direct the whole campaign; the Hebrew idiom rendered "gave judgment" means properly to conduct judicial proceedings against a man, to hear and to sentence him.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, sharpens the legal picture. Nebuchadnezzar was not present at the storming of Jerusalem, as Jer. 39:3 shows, but awaited the result at his base; and the phrase means that he, with the grandees of his court and perhaps some dependent princes, held a solemn trial of Zedekiah as a rebel against his liege lord — a charge in which his breach of a sworn oath would have been made prominent, the very perjury denounced in 2 Chronicles 36:13 and Ezek. 17:15. Ellicott observes that the verb stands in the singular in Jeremiah, throwing the act directly upon the Babylonian king.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley agrees on both points. Riblah was where Nebuchadnezzar stayed, that he might keep the besiegers supplied with men and munitions and yet have an eye on Chaldea to forestall any revolt at home in his absence; and "they" who gave judgment were the king's appointed officers, who examined Zedekiah's cause and passed the sentence about to be described. The witnesses do not contend; together they present the terrible propriety of the moment. The king who had sworn fealty to Babylon by the name of the LORD, and then broken it, is arraigned not in a hot sack of the city but coolly, judicially, at the conqueror's headquarters, so that his fall wears the form of a lawful reckoning. Behind the human tribunal, they let it be felt, stands a higher one: God himself, as Ezekiel had said, had a controversy with Zedekiah for his broken covenant, and the sentence pronounced at Riblah is the earthly echo of that heavenly charge.
7:
The sentence falls in a single dreadful sequence — "they slew the sons of Zedekiah before his eyes, and put out the eyes of Zedekiah, and bound him with fetters of brass, and carried him to Babylon" — and the witnesses read it as the exact and merited harvest of his perjury. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch call the punishment the just reward of the breach of his oath and his hardening himself against the counsel of the LORD, spoken through Jeremiah during the siege, that by a voluntary submission he might have saved himself, his house, the city, and the whole people (Jer. 38:17). His sons, who had fled with him and been taken, together with all the nobles of Judah (Jer. 52:10), were slain before his eyes; then he himself was blinded and led away in double chains of brass, to be kept a prisoner until his death, so that Ezekiel's word in Ezek. 12:13 was fulfilled to the letter.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, adds the historical color. The verbs are singular in Jer. 52:10-11, so that the acts are laid directly to Nebuchadnezzar's order, though the blinding itself need not have been done by his own hand — the Assyrian sculptures actually show kings putting out the eyes of their captives. The putting out of the eyes was a recognized Babylonian punishment, and it was precisely this that gave point to Ezekiel's riddle, that the king should be brought to Babylon and yet not see it though he should die there. The "fetters of brass" are literally a double brass, the manacles and leg-irons pictured on the monuments; and Jeremiah adds that he was put in prison till the day of his death.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws out the cruelty and its purpose: though his sons were but children, they were slain that this last spectacle his eyes should behold might leave an abiding impression of grief and horror on his spirit; and in slaying them the Chaldeans declared in effect that the kingdom was at an end and that neither he nor any of his breed were fit to be trusted, and so not fit to live. Wesley too marks the reconciled prophecies — that he should go to Babylon (Jer. 32:5) and yet never see it — and notes the tragic irony that a false prophet named Zedekiah, unable to reconcile the two, had concluded both were false. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that every stroke of the sentence answers to a word of God long before spoken, so that the blinded king in his brazen chains becomes the living seal of the prophets' truth.
9:
"And he burnt the house of the LORD, and the king's house, and all the houses of Jerusalem" — and the witnesses stand aghast chiefly at the burning of the temple. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch parse the wording, taking "every great house" as a limiting phrase: not every dwelling to the last was fired, but every large house, together with the temple and the royal palaces, while some houses were left standing for the victors' own lodging. Charles Ellicott agrees that the clause "every great man's house" should drop the "man's" and read simply "every great house," matching the "all her palaces" of 2 Chronicles 36:19; the common houses, he adds, were spared for the poor who were left behind (v.12).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the fire into a meditation on judgment and worship. That the king's house and the houses of the great men should burn is no wonder — their inhabitants had by their sins made them combustible; but that the house of the LORD should perish in these flames, that holy and beautiful house bewailed in Isaiah 64:11, which David had prepared for and Solomon built, on which the eye and heart of God had rested — that is strange indeed. Yet it was not fire-proof against God's judgments. By the burning of the temple, Henry says, God would show how little he regards the outward pomp of his worship when the life and power of religion are neglected; the people had trusted in the temple as a charm, crying "the temple of the LORD" in the words rebuked at Jer. 7:4, and now they found it but a refuge of lies. He reckons the house had stood some four hundred and thirty years, and marks the solemn coincidence, recorded by Josephus, that the second temple would be burnt by the Romans in the very same month and day.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley strikes the same note and clears away a legend. One apocryphal writer told how Jeremiah rescued the ark and hid it in a cave on Mount Nebo, but this, Wesley says, is a tale like the rest of that author's, without regard to truth or probability, for Jeremiah was at that time a close prisoner; and the burning of the temple shows how little God cares for the outward pomp of his worship when its life and power are gone. The witnesses do not divide. They agree that the flames which consumed the most sacred house on earth preached a sermon its worshippers had refused to hear: that God will be served in spirit and truth, and that a shrine trusted in place of its God becomes fuel for his judgment.
10:
"And all the army of the Chaldees, that were with the captain of the guard, brake down the walls of Jerusalem round about." The witnesses read the razing of the walls as the completing stroke of the city's ruin. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note that after the burning the whole Chaldean army set to pulling down the walls on every side, and mark a small textual point — that in the parallel a particle has fallen out through a copyist's oversight. Charles Ellicott adds that the preposition "with the captain," though wanting in the common Hebrew text, is preserved in many manuscripts and the old versions and in Jer. 52:14, so that the demolition was carried out by the whole army under Nebuzaradan's command.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke gathers the whole plunder into this moment: in the same fifth month the walls were levelled to the ground, and all who were left in the city, with those who had earlier deserted to Nebuchadnezzar, and the common people, together with the king's treasures and the treasures of the nobles and the whole furniture of the temple, were carried off to Babylon (Jer. 39:8). With this, he reckons, Judah was carried out of her own land some four hundred and sixty-eight years after David began to reign over it, and the fifth age of the world came to its end.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry finds the moral in the ruined defences. The victorious army would be revenged on the walls for having kept them out so long, and would prevent the like resistance another time; but behind their spite Henry sees a deeper cause: sin unwalls a people and takes away their defence. It is a lasting judgment — these walls, he observes, were never repaired until the days of Nehemiah, long after. The witnesses do not divide. Between Keil's grammar, Ellicott's manuscripts, Clarke's inventory of the spoil, and Henry's sober lesson, they render one picture of a city stripped bare: burnt within, breached and levelled without, emptied of its people and its treasure, so that nothing of its old strength remained. The walls that had been Jerusalem's boast are thrown down round about, and the reader is left to see that when God withdraws his favour, no stone stands where sin has undermined the foundation.
12:
"But the captain of the guard left of the poor of the land to be vinedressers and husbandmen." Against the long train of captives the verse sets a small remnant spared, and the witnesses find in it a quiet providence. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note simply that Nebuzaradan left the poorest of the land behind to tend the vineyards and till the fields — the conqueror's practical wish that the country not lie wholly waste. Charles Ellicott adds the philological detail: "the poor of the land" are those already distinguished at the first deportation in 2 Kings 24:14, and the word rendered "husbandmen," occurring here alone in this form, means plowmen, matched by a cognate rarity in Jer. 52:16.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley turns the bare notice into a meditation on the strange reversals of providence: while the rich were prisoners in a strange land, the poor had liberty and peace in their own country — so that Providence sometimes humbles the proud and favours those of low degree. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had struck the very same note, observing that sometimes poverty is a protection, for those who have nothing have nothing to lose; when the wealthy Jews who had oppressed the poor were made strangers and prisoners in an enemy's land, the poor whom they had despised were left at liberty in their own fields.

The witnesses do not divide, and together they draw a gospel of the lowly out of a verse of judgment. The very insignificance that had made these people beneath notice becomes their preservation; the vineyards and cornfields they are left to dress are the one patch of ordinary life spared in a ruined land. There is a rough justice in it, as Henry and Wesley both feel: the great men who had built their houses by oppression are carried off, and the men they had trodden down inherit, for a season, the peace of the countryside. Yet the witnesses do not sentimentalize the mercy; it is a mercy inside a catastrophe, a handful of husbandmen left among the ashes of a kingdom. The land that had flowed with milk and honey is reduced to a labour-gang working for Babylon, and even this remnant, as the coming verses will show, will not hold together, but will scatter in fear to Egypt when its brief peace is broken. For now, though, the poor keep their homes, and Providence writes, even in the margin of ruin, its old lesson that God resists the proud and gives grace to the humble.
14:
"And the pots, and the shovels, and the snuffers, and the spoons, and all the vessels of brass wherewith they ministered, took they away." The witnesses catalogue the smaller furniture of the sanctuary and hear in the inventory the silencing of the temple's daily service. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch simply carry the list forward from the shattered pillars to these ministering vessels of brass, part of the same systematic stripping of the house recorded more fully in Jer. 52:18. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, notes that Jeremiah's parallel adds the sprinkling-bowls and is throughout more detailed than the account here; the phrase "wherewith they ministered" he glosses "used to minister," and refers the several implements to the service of the brazen altar, pointing back to their first making in 1 Kings 7:40.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the bare list into its meaning: the carrying away of the vessels with which they ministered put an end to the ministration itself. It was a righteous thing with God, he says, to deprive of the benefit of his worship those who had so long slighted it and preferred false worships before it; those who would have many altars should now have none. The removal of pot and shovel and censer is thus, in Henry's reading, no mere loss of property but the closing of the sanctuary — the fire on the altar quenched, the daily offering ended, the whole apparatus of approach to God taken out of Judah's hands.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement gives the humble hardware of the temple a terrible eloquence. These were the tools of nearness to God: the pots that bore away the ashes, the shovels and snuffers that tended the lamps, the spoons that carried the incense. While they served, the LORD was daily sought and daily worshipped; taken away, they leave the courts empty and the ordinances suspended. The grammatical-historical witnesses fix what each vessel was and where it is more fully listed; the puritan witness tells what their loss meant — that a people who had turned the worship of God into an empty form now lose even the form. The very completeness of the plunder, sweeping up the least utensil of the altar, measures how entirely the service of the house of God was brought to an end, until, as later history would show, a purified remnant should carry such vessels home again.
19:
The list of the condemned continues with the officers of the city and the palace — "an officer that was set over the men of war, and five men of them that were in the king's presence... the principal scribe of the host... and threescore men of the people of the land." The witnesses attend to their ranks and to a stubborn discrepancy of number. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify the first as a king's chamberlain who commanded the men of war, the "five men who saw the king's face" as his most intimate counsellors, and the scribe as the officer of the commander-in-chief who mustered and enrolled the people for military service. They note that Jer. 52:25 reads seven where Kings has five.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke faces that discrepancy directly. Where this verse says five men of the king's presence, Jer. 52:25 and the Arabic say seven, and the Chaldee even fifty; but since Jeremiah and nearly all the versions read seven, Clarke suggests either that they were but five at first, or that Jeremiah reckoned in with the five the officer over the men of war and the principal scribe, and so made seven in all. Charles Ellicott, of the same school as Keil, takes "the city" to mean specifically the city of David, the strategic citadel; the officer he takes for the commander of its garrison, the five for royal counsellors who may have dissuaded the king from flight and so held out to the last, and the sixty men of the people for the surviving remnant of the citadel's defenders — those, Keil thought, who had most distinguished themselves in the defence, or been ringleaders in the revolt. Ellicott explains the numeral confusion by supposing the letter denoting "five" had been partly obliterated in the manuscript from which Jeremiah's copy descended.

The witnesses do not finally divide; between them they resolve the puzzle of five and seven and agree on the substance. This was a deliberate decapitation of the state — the general, the privy councillors, the muster-master, and the last brave men of the garrison, singled out from the survivors and reserved for execution. The grammatical-historical witnesses fix their offices; the wesleyan-arminian voice reconciles the counts; all see in the roll-call the systematic removal of everyone who had led the city's defence or shared its counsels. What the sword had done to the priests at the altar it now does to the men of war and the men of the pen alike, so that Judah is stripped not only of her temple and her people but of her every remaining head, and left without priest, prince, or captain to answer for her.
20:
"And Nebuzaradan captain of the guard took these, and brought them to the king of Babylon to Riblah." The verse is only a hinge between the seizing of the captives and their execution, yet the witnesses do not pass it by without weight. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch note plainly that the chief of the bodyguard gathered up the officers and priests and men named in the foregoing verses and led them north to Nebuchadnezzar's headquarters at Riblah in the land of Hamath — the same station where the king had already brought Zedekiah to trial. The prisoners are carried out of the ruined city, away from the ashes of the temple, along the very road that had led their own king to his blinding.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry, following these captives to Riblah, feels the cruelty of the reprieve that was no reprieve. Being persons of rank, spared in the sack and led away alive, they might in reason have hoped that surely the bitterness of death was now past — that men brought so far, and kept, were being kept to live. Yet, as the next verse will show, they were brought north only to be slain; the journey to Riblah was not a deliverance but a procession to the block. Henry lets the reader share the captives' false hope precisely to sharpen the horror of its disappointment.

The witnesses do not divide, and the short verse gathers a somber force from its very brevity. Riblah has already become, in this chapter, the place of judgment: there Zedekiah saw his sons killed and lost his own eyes; there now the heads of the nation are brought in their turn. The repetition is deliberate. The historian marches priest and prince and captain up the same road, to the same royal presence, so that the reader understands the fall of Jerusalem to be no chaos of random slaughter but an ordered, deliberate reckoning, carried out at the conqueror's chosen seat. Keil fixes the place and its meaning; Henry fixes the captives' state of mind; between them the verse becomes the tense, quiet pause before the sentence falls — the captives standing at last before the king of Babylon, the whole surviving leadership of Judah assembled in one condemned company at Riblah, waiting to learn that the mercy of a long journey spared alive was only the prelude to a colder death.
23:
"And when all the captains of the armies... heard that the king of Babylon had made Gedaliah governor, there came to Gedaliah to Mizpah" — and the witnesses name the scattered soldiers who now emerged to gather about him. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch list them as they stand: Ishmael son of Nethaniah, Johanan son of Careah, Seraiah the Netophathite, and Jaazaniah the Maachathite, with their men — captains who had escaped the fall and now sought the new governor's protection. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, fills in their histories. These were the army captains who had fled with the king and dispersed over the country, as Jer. 40:7 tells, now come out of hiding. Ishmael he marks as a grandson of Elishama the royal secretary and himself of royal blood (Jer. 41:1) — a pedigree that will prove ominous. Jaazaniah's father was an alien, of the Syrian state of Maachah named in 2 Samuel 10:6.

Ellicott gives particular care to Mizpah itself, and to why Gedaliah chose it. It was a naturally strong position, lying high; and it had been from ancient times the seat of a sanctuary, the gathering-place of Israel in Judges 20:1, so that it might serve in some sort as a substitute for the destroyed temple of Jerusalem, whither men still came with offerings, as Jer. 41:5 shows. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, less certain of its location, notes only that there were several places of the name and that this Mizpah was thought to lie conveniently for one who must receive his instructions from Babylon.

The witnesses do not divide, and their careful roll of names carries a quiet foreboding. For a moment the verse reads like a healing: the broken armies of Judah, scattered when the king fled, come in from their hiding-places to rally round a lawful governor at a hallowed site. Yet the witnesses, especially Ellicott, cannot list these captains without marking the one among them of royal seed, Ishmael, whose coming to Mizpah wears the face of submission but hides the heart of a rival. The gathering that looks like the first knitting-together of a shattered people is, in their reading, the very gathering in which its destroyer is welcomed. Gedaliah opens his door to them all, the loyal and the treacherous alike; and the sanctuary-town that offered a substitute altar will soon be stained with a governor's blood.
28:
"And he spake kindly to him, and set his throne above the throne of the kings that were with him in Babylon." The witnesses dwell on the honour shown the released captive and its careful sense. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch warn against pressing the words too literally: Evil-merodach spoke kindly to Jehoiachin and gave him a place of higher honour than the other captive kings — not necessarily a physically loftier seat, but the more honourable station. The "kings that were with him," they explain, were dethroned monarchs kept at the Babylonian court to swell its splendour, just as Cyrus afterward kept the conquered Croesus by his side. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, reads it much the same way: Jehoiachin was given precedence over the other royal captives who graced the conqueror's hall, a precedence probably marked by allowing him a higher chair of state; and he too recalls how Cyrus honoured Croesus of Lydia, adding the later parallel of the Black Prince's chivalrous courtesy toward his royal prisoner, John of France.

The witnesses do not divide, and their historical comparisons open the meaning of the scene. Jehoiachin is not merely let out of prison; he is set at the head of a company of fallen kings, given the first place among the trophies of Babylon. There is a strange dignity in it, and the witnesses feel the poignancy: the heir of David, who might have reigned in Jerusalem, sits crowned with an alien honour in the city of his captivity, first among the defeated. Yet both Keil and Ellicott let the honour stand as genuine kindness rather than mockery, a real elevation granted by a new king's grace.

Beneath the courtly detail the witnesses sense the quiet hand of providence. That the Davidic king should be singled out for favour among all the captive monarchs of the East is, in the setting of the book's close, no idle court ceremony. The line through which the promises ran is publicly honoured in exile, lifted above thrones that had fallen with it, kept visible and dignified in the very heart of Babylon. The grammatical-historical witnesses are content to fix what the honour was and to compare it with the customs of other conquerors; but the placing of the verse, at the end of a book that has traced the near-extinction of David's house, gives the elevated seat of Jehoiachin a weight beyond its ceremony. The kindly word and the higher throne are the outward signs that the royal line survives, cherished and set on high even where it seemed most utterly overthrown, so that hope for the house of David is kept alive at the table of its captor.
29:
"And changed his prison garments: and he did eat bread continually before him all the days of his life." The witnesses read the change of clothing and the standing place at the royal table as the visible tokens of Jehoiachin's new estate. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that Jehoiachin put off his prison garments and put on other, kingly clothing, just as Joseph had done when raised from the dungeon to Pharaoh's court (Genesis 41:42); and that "he did eat bread continually before him" means he ate at the king's own table, as Mephibosheth once ate at David's (2 Samuel 9:7). More than this, they note, a daily allowance was granted him for the maintenance of the retainers who formed his little court. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, reads the verse the same way: Jehoiachin discarded the garments of his imprisonment for clothing suited to his new condition — again the parallel of Joseph taken from prison to the Egyptian court (Genesis 41:14) — and became a perpetual guest at the royal board, as Mephibosheth had been at the table of the king.

The witnesses do not divide, and their two Old Testament echoes give the verse its depth. In Joseph they see the prisoner suddenly clothed and exalted; in Mephibosheth, the crippled heir of a fallen house taken up to eat continually at the king's table by an act of pure kindness. Both patterns bear on Jehoiachin. Like Joseph, he exchanges the rags of the dungeon for the dress of honour; like Mephibosheth, the last remnant of a broken royal line is granted a settled place at the table of the reigning king, fed daily, not as a prisoner on a ration but as a guest in the household.

The change of garment, in the witnesses' reading, is more than comfort. It marks a change of status made permanent — "all the days of his life" — a lasting reversal of the shame under which the captive king had lain for thirty-seven years. The grammatical-historical witnesses are careful to keep the honour concrete and provable, tying it to the customs of oriental courts and to the plain sense of eating before the king; yet even their sober notes cannot quite hide the tenderness of the scene. The heir of David, so long shut up in disgrace, is dressed anew and seated at the table of his conqueror for the rest of his days. The prison clothes are laid aside for good, and the man who had known nothing but confinement is given, at the last, the daily bread of a king's friend — a quiet emblem, set at the book's very end, of favour breaking in upon long affliction.
30:
The book closes on a note of daily, faithful provision — "his allowance was a continual allowance given him of the king, a daily rate for every day, all the days of his life." The witnesses gather at this last verse to weigh what such a quiet ending means. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott takes the allowance as the maintenance of Jehoiachin's little court, rendering it "a continual allowance was given him from the king, a day's portion in its day." He observes that the writer evidently dwells with pleasure on this faint gleam of light amid the darkness of the exile — a kind of foreshadowing of the pity that would afterward be shown the whole captive people, once the exile had done its work of chastisement and purification, the mercy remembered in Ps. 106:46 and Ezra 9:9. He adds, soberly, that Jehoiachin may well have died before Evil-merodach was murdered, which would be no strange thing given his age and his thirty-seven years of imprisonment.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fills in that end: Jehoiachin lived now in regal style, keeping a kind of court in Babylon, supplied with every requisite by the king's friendship; but about two years later Evil-merodach was slain in a conspiracy, and Clarke supposes that Jehoiachin, then near fifty-eight, may have fallen with his friend and protector — and so, he says, terminates the whole catastrophe of the Jewish kings and people and state. John Wesley draws from the same verse a word of comfort: let none say they shall never see good again because they have long seen little but evil, for the most afflicted know not what blessed turn Providence may yet give to their affairs.

The witnesses do not divide, and their closing reflections turn a mere pension-record into a doorway of hope. Keil and Delitzsch had noted that the historian follows Jehoiachin to the very end of his life on purpose, to show how the captive king fared from his elevation to his death; and all the witnesses feel the deliberateness of the ending. A book that began with the glory of Solomon's kingdom and has ended in ashes, exile, and flight to Egypt sets its last words not on ruin but on a daily loaf given to a freed king at a foreign table. The grammatical-historical witnesses read in it a foreshadowing of the nation's own restoration; the wesleyan-arminian witnesses read in it a promise to every afflicted soul. Together they leave the reader where the historian meant to — with the line of David alive, sustained day by day at the hand of God working even through a heathen king, and the long night of the exile lit, at its close, by one small and steady light.
5:
"And the army of the Chaldees pursued after the king, and overtook him in the plains of Jericho: and all his army were scattered from him." The witnesses gather here around the swift undoing of Zedekiah's flight and the prophecies it fulfilled. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke tells the sequel in a single grim sweep: Zedekiah was taken and brought captive to Riblah in Syria, where Nebuchadnezzar then lay; there his sons were slain before his face and his own eyes put out, and, loaded with chains, he was sent to Babylon (Jer. 39:4). In this, Clarke insists, two seemingly contrary prophecies were reconciled at once — that his eyes should see the eyes of the king of Babylon (Jer. 32:4), and yet that he should not see Babylon though he would die there (Ezek. 12:13).

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott lingers over the geography that made the capture certain. Near Jericho the Arabah widens to eleven or twelve miles; the tract west of the Jordan was called the "plains," or Arboth, of Jericho, while that on the eastern bank bore the name of the plains of Moab (Joshua 4:13; Num. 22:1). It was in that open, unsheltered country, with the Jordan still to cross, that the fleeing king was run to ground. Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, had already fixed the scene in the steppes of Jericho and marked the pathos of the moment: the army that should have defended him melted away, every man shifting for himself, so that the king stood alone before his pursuers.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement makes the verse a study in the futility of flight from God. Zedekiah had refused the one road of safety, surrender at the word of Jeremiah; he chose instead the road toward the plain, and it led him straight into the enemy's hand. The scattering of his guard, in their common reading, is the outward sign of a defence long since withdrawn from within: had he put himself under the protection of God, that protection would not have failed him now. Instead the very speed of the Chaldean pursuit becomes the instrument by which the prophetic word is kept to the letter, and the last king of David's line to reign in Jerusalem is carried off, disarmed and alone, to the judgment prepared for him at Riblah. The plains of Jericho, where Israel had first entered the land in triumph under Joshua, become the place where her last king is taken out of it in chains.
21:
"And the king of Babylon smote them, and slew them at Riblah in the land of Hamath. So Judah was carried away out of their land." The witnesses read the double sentence — the execution of the leaders and the summary epitaph of the nation — as the closing of an era. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the king's severity: he had no doubt learned that these were the men who had counselled Zedekiah to revolt, and so struck them down as the authors of the rebellion. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott adds that Nebuchadnezzar was too irritated by the obstinacy of the city's defence to admire the bravery of its defenders; and he takes the concluding clause, "so Judah was carried away out of their land," to round off not merely Nebuzaradan's proceedings but the whole account of the destruction and deportation, echoing the like verdict once passed on the northern kingdom in 2 Kings 17:23. He recalls too that the prophecy of Obadiah rebukes the heartless glee of the Edomites at Judah's ruin, the cruelty lamented in Ps. 137:7 and Lam. 4:21-22.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry stands longest over the epitaph. These men, spared through the sack and the long march, were now all put to death when in reason they might have hoped the worst was over; and with their slaughter the calamity was complete — Judah carried out of the land some eight hundred and sixty years after Joshua had put them in possession of it. Now, Henry says, the ancient threat was fulfilled to the letter, that the LORD would bring them and the king they set over them into a nation they had not known (Deut. 28:36); and the word of Amos 3:2 was made good, that because God had known Israel alone of all the families of the earth, therefore he would punish them for their iniquities.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley closes with the same reckoning, that the slaying of the leaders completed the calamity, and Judah's tenure of the land, begun under Joshua, was ended after some eight and a half centuries. The witnesses do not divide. Between Clarke's political motive, Ellicott's wider verdict, and Henry's and Wesley's long backward look, they make this verse the formal death of the kingdom of Judah — the leaders slain, the people gone, the covenant threats fulfilled — and set over the whole a single grave sentence that had once been written over the sister kingdom too: so Judah was carried away out of their own land.
24:
"And Gedaliah sware to them, and to their men, and said unto them, Fear not to be the servants of the Chaldees: dwell in the land, and serve the king of Babylon; and it shall be well with you." The witnesses read the governor's oath as a wise and generous pledge, and weigh its exact sense. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that these captains feared the vengeance of the Chaldeans because they had fought against them, and that Gedaliah therefore assured them on oath that they had nothing to dread if they would settle peaceably, submit to the king of Babylon, and cultivate the land, as Jer. 40:9 more fully records. Charles Ellicott sharpens the wording: the phrase is better rendered "be not afraid of the servants of the Chaldees," by whom Gedaliah means those, himself among them, who now owned the Chaldeans as masters; he promises the captains full immunity for the past if only they will settle quietly as subjects of the conqueror.

The wesleyan-arminian voices dwell on the ground of the oath and the tragedy hanging over it. Adam Clarke notes simply that Gedaliah pledged himself in the most solemn manner to encourage and protect them. John Wesley goes further: Gedaliah could safely swear that they should be kept from the evils they feared, because he had not only the king of Babylon's promise but also God's own promise delivered by Jeremiah. For a moment, Wesley says, a fair prospect seemed to be opening again — and then he breaks off with a lament for how soon the scene would change, this hopeful settlement quickly dashed in pieces, not by the Chaldeans, but by some of the people's own.

The witnesses do not divide, and their comment turns the verse into a picture of counsel given in vain. Gedaliah offers exactly what the prophets had urged — submission, patience, ordinary labour under a foreign yoke, and with it life and peace. His oath rests on a double security, the word of the conqueror and the word of God through Jeremiah, so that no promise could be surer. The grammatical-historical witnesses fix its terms; the wesleyan-arminian witnesses fix its firmness and then, in the same breath, its coming ruin. For the reader already knows, and Wesley will not let him forget, that this well-meant pledge of safety will be answered with treachery: the governor who swears to shield his people will be cut down by one of the very captains to whom he opens his hand, and the peace he offered will be lost, as so much else in this book, by the folly and violence of Judah herself.
11:
"Now the rest of the people that were left in the city, and the fugitives that fell away to the king of Babylon, with the remnant of the multitude, did Nebuzaradan the captain of the guard carry away." The witnesses attend to who exactly was swept off, and to a knot in the Hebrew. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch distinguish three groups led away — those left in the city, the deserters who had gone over to the Chaldeans, and the remnant of the multitude — and weigh a variant reading that would make the last phrase "the remnant of the artisans," the smiths and craftsmen of 2 Kings 24:14; but they judge it precluded by the wording of Jer. 39:9. Charles Ellicott glosses the same terms: "the fugitives that fell away" are the deserters who had heeded Jeremiah's counsel to go out to the besiegers (Jer. 38:2), and "the multitude" is a word meaning a shouting throng, probably the rank and file of the fighting men.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley puts numbers and faces to the deportation. Those whom neither sword nor famine had destroyed — some eight hundred and thirty-two persons, he reckons from Jer. 52:29, the settled inhabitants and traders of the city — were carried into exile, while the "multitude" names the country people who had crowded in. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had drawn the same tally into a reflection: most of the inhabitants had already perished by sword or famine or had scattered when the king fled, so that only this small remnant, with the deserters, was left to be led to Babylon.

The witnesses do not divide, and their care over the lists is not pedantry but pathos. What had been a teeming city is reduced to a column of captives whose number can be written down; the very precision of the count — deserters, survivors, the shouting throng, eight hundred and thirty-two souls — measures how total the emptying was. Those who had trusted their walls, their treasures, and their alliance with Egypt are marched away in chains, and the historian, by naming each class that went, lets the reader feel the weight of a judgment that spared neither the soldier who fought to the last nor the deserter who gave himself up. Only the question of who remained behind, taken up in the next verse, keeps the picture from being one of utter desolation.
15:
"And the firepans, and the bowls, and such things as were of gold, in gold, and of silver, in silver, the captain of the guard took away." The witnesses turn to the precious vessels of the holy place, the last and finest of the spoil. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the fullest account. The firepans and bowls are of the sort first made under Solomon and listed in 1 Kings 7:50, and Jeremiah's parallel names five other kinds of vessel besides; the clause "such things as were of gold, in gold, and of silver, in silver" he takes as a sweeping expression meant to gather up every remaining object of those two metals. These, he notes, were the utensils of the holy place itself, and many of them had doubtless been carefully hidden away by the priests at the first plundering of the temple under Jehoiachin (2 Kings 24:13), only to be found and seized now.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, observe that the notice here is abridged in comparison with the fuller enumeration of Jer. 52:19, the writer of Kings naming only the firepans and the bowls where Jeremiah lists more, and marking that the smaller vessels of gold and silver were taken away whole, being small enough to carry, while the great brazen furniture had to be broken up. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry had already gathered the point of it: some things of gold and silver still remained, most of the finer sort having been carried off at the earlier deportation, and now these too were swept away, so that the house was left utterly bare.

The witnesses do not divide, and their common reading gives this closing inventory a mournful thoroughness. What the fire could not consume, the captain of the guard carried off; what the priests had concealed in hope of better days was hunted out and taken; gold and silver, firepan and bowl, all followed the broken bronze to Babylon. The distinction the text makes — the great vessels shattered, the small ones borne away entire — only underlines how nothing was left. A sanctuary that had gleamed with the offerings of David and Solomon is stripped to the walls that are themselves about to fall. In the witnesses' telling, the removal of the last golden bowl is the final emptying of the house of God, the visible sign that the glory had departed, and that the treasures once devoted to his worship had become the trophies of a heathen king.
16:
"The two pillars, one sea, and the bases which Solomon had made for the house of the LORD; the brass of all these vessels was without weight." The verse pauses to sum up the sheer mass of metal carried off, and the witnesses dwell on that closing hyperbole. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the opening words as a nominative absolute — the two pillars, the one sea, the bases — resumed by "all these vessels," and takes "without weight" as a natural exaggeration, closely resembling a phrase that recurs in the Assyrian records of plunder, where a conqueror boasts of carrying off "spoils without number." The point is not a literal impossibility of weighing but the overwhelming quantity of the bronze that Solomon had lavished on the house.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, had listed these same great objects — the two pillars, the molten sea, the ten stands or bases — as the colossal furnishings that had to be broken up before they could be moved, all of them the work of Solomon for the temple. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry seizes on the same detail of the immeasurable brass: most of this plunder, he observes, was bronze, and so vast a quantity of it that the text says it was without weight; the silver and gold were mostly gone before, but the brass remained to be hauled away by the ton.

The witnesses do not divide, and the verse's backward glance to Solomon gives their comment its weight. "Which Solomon had made for the house of the LORD" — the very phrase measures the fall. What the wisest and wealthiest of Israel's kings had cast in the plain of Jordan for the glory of God, at a cost the chronicler could scarcely reckon, is now bronze beyond weighing in a Chaldean baggage-train. The grammatical-historical witnesses set the boast of "without weight" beside the pride of pagan conquerors, and the effect is bitter: the treasures of the temple are counted now in the same breath as any heathen loot. Henry lets the irony stand plainly — the house that had once received the offerings of a devout king is emptied by an idolatrous one, and the abundance that had been Israel's glory becomes the measure of her spoil. The pillars that upheld nothing structural, but stood as monuments to God's establishing strength, are dragged away in fragments too heavy to number; and the reader is meant to feel, in that unweighable brass, the full mass of a glory departed.
17:
The verse lingers over the measurements of the ruined pillars — "the height of the one pillar was eighteen cubits, and the chapiter upon it was brass... and the wreathen work, and pomegranates upon the chapiter round about, all of brass" — as if to record for memory the beauty now broken up. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott gives the verse close attention, chiefly to correct its numbers and terms. The "three cubits" assigned here to the height of the capital he judges an error of transcription for "five," the figure preserved in the original description of 1 Kings 7:16 and in the parallels of Jer. 52:22 and 2 Chronicles 3:15. The "wreathen work" he renders lattice-work, the net-like ornament described in 1 Kings 7:17, and the pomegranates he takes to have hung upon that lattice; the whole notice, he observes, is much abridged, a fuller sentence surviving in Jeremiah that counts the pomegranates at ninety-six exposed to view, a hundred in all around the network.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same school, had gathered these same details into their running list of the temple bronze — the two pillars each eighteen cubits high, crowned with their capitals and wreathen work and pomegranates, all of one metal — as part of the systematic record of what the Chaldeans destroyed and carried away. Between the two witnesses the verse is treated as a careful, almost reverent, inventory of a lost magnificence, its very figures worth correcting because the thing described was worth remembering.

The witnesses do not divide, and their patient work over cubits and lattices carries its own quiet grief. The historian, they suggest, does not merely note that the pillars were taken; he sets down their height, their crowns, their hundred pomegranates, as one records the features of a face about to be lost. Every measurement is a memorial. The pillars that had framed the entrance to the house of God, adorned with the wreathed nets and the ripe pomegranates that spoke of fruitfulness and life, are here described in the past tense of destruction, their proportions preserved on the page just as their substance is broken up for Babylon. In the witnesses' reading, the very exactness of the account is an act of remembrance: Israel's most sacred architecture is entered into the record at the moment of its ruin, so that what the Chaldeans could shatter and carry off should not be wholly forgotten by the people whose fathers had raised it to the glory of God.
